justification: All pass criteria met: 8 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (well beyond the 3 minimum), covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), delivered rejection (AC-3), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-4), authorization (AC-5), atomicity failure (AC-6), audit trail (AC-7), and post-dispatch blocked (AC-8). Multiple ambiguities surfaced in both low_confidence_areas (5 items) and escalations (3 detailed escalations covering post-dispatch policy, partial cancellation, and refund policy). Confidence level of 45 is well below the 65 threshold. Edge cases section is richly populated with 8 scenarios. No business rules are invented as facts — all uncertain areas are explicitly flagged as undefined or escalated.

observations.text: 8 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, authorization, atomicity, and audit trail
Confidence level of 45 correctly reflects the underspecified single-sentence input
3 detailed escalations with options and recommendations — post-dispatch policy, partial cancellation, and refund policy
5 low_confidence_areas explicitly surface ambiguities including post-dispatch policy, refund details, partial cancellation, fees, and loyalty point reversal
BR-6 and AC-8 correctly mark post-dispatch cancellation as UNDEFINED/BLOCKED rather than inventing a rule
Edge cases section covers race conditions, TOCTOU, partial payment, promotions, inventory, timeouts, and concurrent state transitions
Who-can-cancel question is addressed via BR-1 and AC-5, though framed as an invariant rather than flagged as an assumption from the underspecified input

BR-1 asserts only the order owner can cancel as an invariant, but the spec never specified this — it should have been flagged as an assumption or escalated as an ambiguity about whether operations/support staff can also cancel
Business impact analysis is thorough with stakeholder identification across customers, finance, warehouse, support, and courier partners
